([endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): *suomi* [[ˈsuo̯mi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Finnish "Help:IPA/Finnish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fi-suomi.ogg "File:Fi-suomi.ogg") or *suomen kieli* [[ˈsuo̯meŋ ˈkie̯li]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Finnish "Help:IPA/Finnish")) is a [Finnic language](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") of the [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") language family, spoken by the majority of the population in [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") and by ethnic Finns outside of Finland. Finnish is one of the two [official languages](/wiki/Official_languages "Official languages") of Finland, alongside [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"). In [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), both Finnish and [Meänkieli](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nkieli "Meänkieli") (which has significant [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with Finnish[[5]](#cite_note-5)) are official [minority languages](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language"). [Kven](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language"), which like Meänkieli is mutually intelligible with Finnish, is spoken in the [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway "Norway") counties of [Troms](/wiki/Troms "Troms") and [Finnmark](/wiki/Finnmark "Finnmark") by a minority of Finnish descent.

**Finnish** ([endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): *suomi* [[ˈsuo̯mi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Finnish "Help:IPA/Finnish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fi-suomi.ogg "File:Fi-suomi.ogg") or *suomen kieli* [[ˈsuo̯meŋ ˈkie̯li]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Finnish "Help:IPA/Finnish")) is a [Finnic language](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") of the [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") language family, spoken by the majority of the population in [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") and by ethnic Finns outside of Finland. Finnish is one of the two [official languages](/wiki/Official_languages "Official languages") of Finland, alongside [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"). In [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), both Finnish and [Meänkieli](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nkieli "Meänkieli") (which has significant [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with Finnish[[5]](#cite_note-5)) are official [minority languages](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language"). [Kven](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language"), which like Meänkieli is mutually intelligible with Finnish, is spoken in the [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway "Norway") counties of [Troms](/wiki/Troms "Troms") and [Finnmark](/wiki/Finnmark "Finnmark") by a minority of Finnish descent.

Finnish is [typologically](/wiki/Morphological_typology "Morphological typology") [agglutinative](/wiki/Agglutinative_language "Agglutinative language")[[6]](#cite_note-6) and uses almost exclusively [suffixal affixation](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix"). [Nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun"), [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective"), [pronouns](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun"), [numerals](/wiki/Numeral_(linguistics) "Numeral (linguistics)") and [verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb") are [inflected](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") depending on their role in the [sentence](/wiki/Sentence_(linguistics) "Sentence (linguistics)"). Sentences are normally formed with [subject–verb–object](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object") word order, although the extensive use of inflection allows them to be ordered differently. Word order variations are often reserved for differences in [information structure](/wiki/Information_structure "Information structure").[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Finnish orthography](/wiki/Finnish_orthography "Finnish orthography") uses a Latin-script alphabet derived from the Swedish alphabet, and is [phonemic](/wiki/Phonetic_orthography "Phonetic orthography") to a great extent. [Vowel length](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length") and [consonant length](/wiki/Gemination "Gemination") are distinguished, and there are a range of [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthongs "Diphthongs"), although [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony") limits which diphthongs are possible.

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Classification")]

Finnish belongs to the [Finnic](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") branch of the [Uralic language family](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages"); as such, it is one of the few European languages that is not [Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"). The Finnic branch also includes [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language") and a few minority languages spoken around the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") and in Russia's [Republic of Karelia](/wiki/Republic_of_Karelia "Republic of Karelia"). The closest relative of Finnish is either [Ingrian](/wiki/Ingrian_language "Ingrian language"), or depending on the definition, [Karelian](/wiki/Karelian_language "Karelian language"). Finnic languages form a dialect continuum, where for instance Finnish and Estonian are not separated by any single [isogloss](/wiki/Isogloss "Isogloss") that would separate dialects considered "Finnish" from those considered "Estonian", despite the two standard languages being not mutually intelligible.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Finnish demonstrates an affiliation with other [Uralic languages](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") (such as [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") and [Sami languages](/wiki/Sami_languages "Sami languages")) in several respects including:

* Shared morphology:
  + case suffixes such as [genitive](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") *-n*, [partitive](/wiki/Partitive_case "Partitive case") *-(t)a* / *-(t)ä* ( < [Proto-Uralic](/wiki/Proto-Uralic "Proto-Uralic") \**-ta*, originally [ablative](/wiki/Ablative_case "Ablative case")), [essive](/wiki/Essive_case "Essive case") *-na* / *-nä* ( < \**-na*, originally [locative](/wiki/Locative_case "Locative case"))
  + plural markers *-t* and *-i-* ( < Proto-Uralic \**-t* and \**-j*, respectively)
  + possessive suffixes such as 1st person singular *-ni* ( < Proto-Uralic \**-n-mi*), 2nd person singular *-si* ( < Proto-Uralic \**-ti*).
  + various derivational suffixes (e.g. [causative](/wiki/Causative "Causative") *-tta/-ttä* < Proto-Uralic \**-kta*)
* Shared basic vocabulary displaying regular sound correspondences with the other Uralic languages (e.g. *kala* 'fish' ~ [North Saami](/wiki/North_Saami_language "North Saami language") *guolli* ~ [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") *hal*; and *kadota* 'disappear' ~ North Saami *guođđit* ~ Hungarian *hagy* 'leave (behind)'.

Several theories exist as to the geographic origin of the most recent common ancestor of Finnish and the other Uralic languages ([Proto-Uralic](/wiki/Proto-Uralic "Proto-Uralic")). The most widely held view is that it originated somewhere in the [boreal forest](/wiki/Boreal_forest "Boreal forest") belt around the [Ural Mountains](/wiki/Ural_Mountains "Ural Mountains") region and/or the bend of the middle [Volga](/wiki/Volga "Volga"). The strong case for Proto-Uralic is supported by common vocabulary with regularities in sound correspondences, as well as by the fact that the Uralic languages have many similarities in structure and grammar.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Despite having overlapping geographical distributions, Finnic languages and [Sami languages](/wiki/Sami_languages "Sami languages") are not closely related, and the hypothesis of a separate taxonomic "[Finno-Samic](/wiki/Finno-Samic_languages "Finno-Samic languages")" node is controversial.

The [Defense Language Institute](/wiki/Defense_Language_Institute "Defense Language Institute") in [Monterey, California](/wiki/Monterey,_California "Monterey, California"), United States, classifies Finnish as a level III language (of four levels) in terms of learning difficulty for native English speakers.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Suomenkieliset_Suomessa.png/250px-Suomenkieliset_Suomessa.png)](/wiki/File:Suomenkieliset_Suomessa.png)

Share of Finnish speakers in the population of [municipalities of Finland](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Finland "Municipalities of Finland") in 2020.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Finsktalande_s%C3%B6dra_sverige.svg/250px-Finsktalande_s%C3%B6dra_sverige.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Finsktalande_s%C3%B6dra_sverige.svg)

Areas in Central and Southern Sweden with a Finnish-speaking population (2005)

Finnish is spoken by about five million people, most of whom reside in Finland. There are also notable Finnish-speaking minorities in Sweden, Norway, Russia, Estonia, Brazil, Canada, and the United States. The majority of the population of Finland (90.37% as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit)[[12]](#cite_note-lang_stats-12)) speak Finnish as their [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language"). The remainder speak [Swedish](/wiki/Finland_Swedish "Finland Swedish") (5.42%),[[12]](#cite_note-lang_stats-12) one of the [Sámi](/wiki/Sami_languages "Sami languages") languages (for example [Northern](/wiki/Northern_Sami "Northern Sami"), [Inari](/wiki/Inari_S%C3%A1mi_language "Inari Sámi language"), or [Skolt](/wiki/Skolt_Sami_language "Skolt Sami language")), or another language as their first language. Finnish is spoken as a second language in Estonia by about 167,000 people.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The Finnic varieties found in Norway's [Finnmark](/wiki/Finnmark "Finnmark") (namely [Kven](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language")) and in northern Sweden (namely [Meänkieli](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nkieli "Meänkieli")) have the status of official minority languages, and thus can be considered distinct languages from Finnish. However, since these languages are [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility"), one may alternatively view them as [dialects of the same language](/wiki/Dialect#Dialect_or_language "Dialect").

No language census exists for Norway, neither for Kven, standard Finnish, or combined. As of 2023, 7,454 first- or second-generation immigrants from Finland were registered as having Norwegian residency,[[14]](#cite_note-14) while as of 2021, 235 Finns were registered as foreigners studying at Norwegian higher education.[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Great Norwegian Encyclopedia](/wiki/Great_Norwegian_Encyclopedia "Great Norwegian Encyclopedia") estimates Kven speakers at 2,000-8,000.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Altogether, this results in a total amount of Finnish-speakers roughly between 7,200 and 15,600.

In the [latest census](/wiki/Russian_Census_(2021) "Russian Census (2021)"), around 1000 people in Russia claimed to speak Finnish natively; however, a larger amount of 14,000 claimed to be able to speak Finnish in total.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

There are also forms of Finnish spoken by diasporas outside Europe, such as [American Finnish](/wiki/American_Finnish "American Finnish"), spoken by [Finnish Americans](/wiki/Finnish_Americans "Finnish Americans"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) and [Siberian Finnish](/wiki/Siberian_Finnish "Siberian Finnish"), spoken by [Siberian Finns](/wiki/Siberian_Finns "Siberian Finns").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Official status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Official status")]

Today, Finnish is one of two [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of Finland (the other being Swedish), and has been an official language of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") since 1995. However, the Finnish language did not have an official status in the country during the [period of Swedish rule](/wiki/Finland_under_Swedish_rule "Finland under Swedish rule"), which ended in 1809. After the establishment of the [Grand Duchy of Finland](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Finland "Grand Duchy of Finland"), and against the backdrop of the [Fennoman movement](/wiki/Fennoman_movement "Fennoman movement"), the language obtained its official status in the [Finnish Diet](/wiki/Diet_of_Finland "Diet of Finland") of 1863.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Finnish also enjoys the status of an official [minority language in Sweden](/wiki/Minority_languages_in_Sweden "Minority languages in Sweden"). Under the [Nordic Language Convention](/wiki/Nordic_Language_Convention "Nordic Language Convention"), citizens of the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") speaking Finnish have the opportunity to use their native language when interacting with official bodies in other Nordic countries without being liable to any interpretation or translation costs.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) However, concerns have been expressed about the future status of Finnish in Sweden, for example, where reports produced for the Swedish government during 2017 show that minority language policies are not being respected, particularly for the 7% of Finns settled in the country.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Finnish language](/wiki/History_of_the_Finnish_language "History of the Finnish language")

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

The [Uralic family](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") of languages, of which Finnish is a member, are hypothesized to derive from a single ancestor language termed [Proto-Uralic](/wiki/Proto-Uralic "Proto-Uralic"), spoken sometime between 8,000 and 2,000 BCE (estimates vary) in the vicinity of the [Ural Mountains](/wiki/Ural_Mountains "Ural Mountains").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Over time, Proto-Uralic split into various [daughter languages](/wiki/Daughter_language "Daughter language"), which themselves continued to change and diverge, yielding yet more descendants. One of these descendants is the [reconstructed](/wiki/Linguistic_reconstruction "Linguistic reconstruction") [Proto-Finnic](/wiki/Proto-Finnic "Proto-Finnic"), from which the [Finnic languages](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") developed.[[25]](#cite_note-Laakso-2001-25)

Current models assume that three or more Proto-Finnic dialects evolved during the first millennium BCE.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[25]](#cite_note-Laakso-2001-25) These dialects were defined geographically, and were distinguished from one another along a north–south split as well as an east–west split. The northern dialects of Proto-Finnic, from which Finnish developed, lacked the mid vowel [[ɤ](/wiki/Close-mid_back_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid back unrounded vowel")]. This vowel was found only in the southern dialects, which developed into [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language"), [Livonian](/wiki/Livonian_language "Livonian language"), and [Votian](/wiki/Votic_language "Votic language").[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Finnish_language#Prehistory "Talk:Finnish language")*] The northern variants used third person singular pronoun *hän* instead of southern *tämä* (Est. *tema*). While the eastern dialects of Proto-Finnic (which developed in the modern-day eastern Finnish dialects, Veps, Karelian, and Ingrian) formed genitive plural nouns via plural stems (e.g., eastern Finnish *kalojen* < \****kaloi**-ten*), the western dialects of Proto-Finnic (today's Estonian, Livonian and western Finnish varieties) used the non-plural stems (e.g., Est. *kalade* < \****kala**-ten*). Another defining characteristic of the east–west split was the use of the [reflexive](/wiki/Reflexive_verb "Reflexive verb") suffix *-(t)te*, used only in the eastern dialects.[[25]](#cite_note-Laakso-2001-25)

### Medieval period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Medieval period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Birch-bark_letter_292_real.jpg/250px-Birch-bark_letter_292_real.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birch-bark_letter_292_real.jpg)

[Birch bark letter no. 292](/wiki/Birch_bark_letter_no._292 "Birch bark letter no. 292") is the oldest known document in any Finnic language.

The [Birch bark letter no. 292](/wiki/Birch_bark_letter_no._292 "Birch bark letter no. 292") from the early 13th century is the first known document in any [Finnic language](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages"); it is written in a variety that is closest to modern [Karelian](/wiki/Karelian_language "Karelian language") or [Veps](/wiki/Veps_language "Veps language").[[27]](#cite_note-27) The first known written example of Finnish itself is found in a German travel journal dating back to c. 1450: *Mÿnna tachton gernast spuho sommen gelen Emÿna daÿda* (Modern Finnish: "*Minä tahdon kernaasti puhua suomen kielen, [mutta] en minä taida;*" English: "I want to speak Finnish, [but] I am not able to").[[28]](#cite_note-28) According to the travel journal, the words are those of a Finnish bishop whose name is unknown. The erroneous use of *gelen* (Modern Finnish *kielen*) in the accusative case, rather than *kieltä* in the partitive, and the lack of the [conjunction](/wiki/Conjunction_(grammar) "Conjunction (grammar)") *mutta* are typical of foreign speakers of Finnish even today.[[29]](#cite_note-29) At the time, most priests in Finland [spoke Swedish](/wiki/Swedish-speaking_Finns "Swedish-speaking Finns").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

During the Middle Ages, when [Finland was under Swedish rule](/wiki/Finland_under_Swedish_rule "Finland under Swedish rule"), Finnish was only [spoken](/wiki/Spoken_language "Spoken language"). At the time, the language of [international commerce](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") was [Middle Low German](/wiki/Middle_Low_German "Middle Low German"), the language of administration [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"), and religious ceremonies were held in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"). This meant that Finnish speakers could use their mother tongue only in everyday life. Finnish was considered inferior to Swedish, and Finnish speakers were second-class members of society because they could not use their language in any official situations. There were even efforts to reduce the use of Finnish through parish clerk schools, the use of Swedish in church, and by having Swedish-speaking servants and maids move to Finnish-speaking areas.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Writing system")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Mikael_Agricola_by_Albert_Edelfelt.jpg/250px-Mikael_Agricola_by_Albert_Edelfelt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mikael_Agricola_by_Albert_Edelfelt.jpg)

[Mikael Agricola](/wiki/Mikael_Agricola "Mikael Agricola"), a 19th-century drawing by [Albert Edelfelt](/wiki/Albert_Edelfelt "Albert Edelfelt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Lonnrot4.jpg/250px-Lonnrot4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lonnrot4.jpg)

[Elias Lönnrot](/wiki/Elias_L%C3%B6nnrot "Elias Lönnrot") as depicted in a 19th-century caricature – Lönnrot made several journeys to Karelia and Eastern Finland to collect folklore, from which he compiled the *[Kalevala](/wiki/Kalevala "Kalevala")*.

The first comprehensive writing system for Finnish was created by [Mikael Agricola](/wiki/Mikael_Agricola "Mikael Agricola"), a Finnish bishop, in the 16th century. He based his writing system on the [western dialects](#Western_dialects). Agricola's ultimate plan was to [translate the Bible](/wiki/Bible_translations "Bible translations"),[[32]](#cite_note-32) but first he had to develop an [orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") for the language, which he based on Swedish, German, and Latin. The Finnish [standard language](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") still relies on his innovations with regard to spelling, though Agricola used less systematic spelling than is used today.[[33]](#cite_note-Nordlund-2012-33)

Though Agricola's intention was that each [phoneme](/wiki/Finnish_phonology "Finnish phonology") (and [allophone](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") under [qualitative consonant gradation](/wiki/Finnish_consonant_gradation#Qualitative_gradation "Finnish consonant gradation")) should correspond to one letter, he failed to achieve this goal in various respects. For example, *k*, *c*, and *q* were all used for the phoneme /[k](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_plosive "Voiceless velar plosive")/. Likewise, he alternated between *dh* and *d* to represent the allophonic [[ð](/wiki/Voiced_dental_fricative "Voiced dental fricative")] (like *th* in English *this*), between *dh* and *z* to represent /[θː](/wiki/Voiceless_dental_fricative "Voiceless dental fricative")/ (like *th* in *thin*, but longer in duration), and between *gh* and *g* to represent the allophonic [[ɣ](/wiki/Voiced_velar_fricative "Voiced velar fricative")]. Agricola did not consistently represent [vowel length](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length") in his orthography.[[33]](#cite_note-Nordlund-2012-33)

Others revised Agricola's work later, striving for a more systematic writing system. Along the way, Finnish lost several [fricative consonants](/wiki/Fricative_consonant "Fricative consonant") in a process of [sound change](/wiki/Sound_change "Sound change"). The sounds [ð] and [θ(ː)] disappeared from the language, surviving only in a small rural region in Western Finland.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In the standard language, however, the effect of the lost sounds is thus:

* [ð] became [d]. The sound [ð] was written ⟨d⟩ or ⟨dh⟩ by Agricola. This sound was lost from most varieties of Finnish, either losing all phonetic realization or being pronounced as [r], [ɾ], [l], or [h] instead (depending on dialect and the position in the word). However, Agricola's spelling ⟨d⟩ prevailed, and the pronunciation in Standard Finnish became [d] through [spelling pronunciation](/wiki/Spelling_pronunciation "Spelling pronunciation").[[33]](#cite_note-Nordlund-2012-33)
* [θː, θ] became [ts]. These interdental fricatives were written as ⟨tz⟩ (for both [grades](/wiki/Finnish_consonant_gradation "Finnish consonant gradation"): geminate and short) in some of the earliest written records. Though these developed into a variety of other sounds depending on dialect ([tː, t], [ht, h], [ht, t], [sː, s], [tː, tː], or [ht, ht]), the standard language has arrived at [spelling pronunciation](/wiki/Spelling_pronunciation "Spelling pronunciation") [ts] (which is treated as a [consonant cluster](/wiki/Consonant_cluster "Consonant cluster") and hence not subject to consonant gradation).
* [ɣ] became:
  + [ʋ] if it appeared originally between [high](/wiki/Vowel_height "Vowel height") [round](/wiki/Roundedness "Roundedness") vowels [u] and [y] (cf. *suku* 'kin, family' : *suvun* [genitive form] from earlier \**suku : \*suɣun*, and *kyky* : *kyvyn* 'ability, skill' [nominative and genitive, respectively] from \**kükü : \*küɣün*, contrasting with *sika* : *sian* 'pig, pork' [nominative and genitive] from \**sika : \*siɣan*. A similar process explains the /f/ pronunciation for some English words with "gh", such as "tough"),
  + [j] between a liquid consonant [l] or [r] and a vowel [e] (like in *kuljen* 'I go', a form of the verb *kulkea* 'to go' that was originally \**kulɣen*),
  + and otherwise it was lost entirely.

Modern Finnish punctuation, along with that of Swedish, uses the [colon](/wiki/Colon_(punctuation) "Colon (punctuation)") (:) to separate the [stem](/wiki/Stem_(linguistics) "Stem (linguistics)") of a word and its grammatical ending in some cases, for example after [acronyms](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym"), as in *EU:ssa* 'in the EU'. (This contrasts with some other alphabetic writing systems, which would use other symbols, such as e.g. apostrophe, hyphen.) Since suffixes play a prominent role in the language, this use of the colon is quite common.

### Modernization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Modernization")]

In the 19th century [Johan Vilhelm Snellman](/wiki/Johan_Vilhelm_Snellman "Johan Vilhelm Snellman") and others began to stress the need to improve the status of Finnish. Ever since the days of Mikael Agricola, written Finnish had been used almost exclusively in religious contexts, but now Snellman's [Hegelian](/wiki/Hegel "Hegel") [nationalistic](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") ideas of Finnish as a fully-fledged national language gained considerable support. Concerted efforts were made to improve the status of the language and to modernize it, and by the end of the century Finnish had become a language of administration, journalism, literature, and science in Finland, along with Swedish.

In 1853 [Daniel Europaeus](/wiki/Daniel_Europaeus "Daniel Europaeus") published the first Swedish-Finnish dictionary,[[35]](#cite_note-35) and between 1866 and 1880 [Elias Lönnrot](/wiki/Elias_L%C3%B6nnrot "Elias Lönnrot") compiled the first Finnish-Swedish dictionary.[[36]](#cite_note-36) In the same period, [Antero Warelius](/wiki/Antero_Warelius "Antero Warelius") conducted ethnographic research and, among other topics, he documented the geographic distribution of the Finnish dialects.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

The most important contributions to improving the status of Finnish were made by [Elias Lönnrot](/wiki/Elias_L%C3%B6nnrot "Elias Lönnrot"). His impact on the development of modern vocabulary in Finnish was particularly significant. In addition to compiling the *[Kalevala](/wiki/Kalevala "Kalevala")*, he acted as an arbiter in disputes about the development of standard Finnish between the proponents of western and eastern dialects, ensuring that the western dialects preferred by Agricola retained their preeminent role, while many originally dialect words from Eastern Finland were introduced to the standard language, thus enriching it considerably.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The first novel written in Finnish (and by a Finnish speaker) was *[Seven Brothers](/wiki/Seven_Brothers "Seven Brothers")* (*Seitsemän veljestä*), published by [Aleksis Kivi](/wiki/Aleksis_Kivi "Aleksis Kivi") in 1870.

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Dialects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Finnish_dialects_and_forms_of_speech.png/500px-Finnish_dialects_and_forms_of_speech.png)](/wiki/File:Finnish_dialects_and_forms_of_speech.png)

Map of Finnish dialects and forms of speech

The dialects of Finnish are divided into two distinct groups, Western and Eastern.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The dialects are largely mutually intelligible and are distinguished from each other by changes in vowels, diphthongs and rhythm, as well as in preferred grammatical constructions. For the most part, the dialects operate on the same phonology and grammar. There are only marginal examples of sounds or grammatical constructions specific to some dialect and not found in standard Finnish. Two examples are the [voiced dental fricative](/wiki/Voiced_dental_fricative "Voiced dental fricative") found in the [Rauma dialect](/wiki/Rauma_dialect "Rauma dialect"), and the Eastern [exessive case](/wiki/Exessive_case "Exessive case").

### Western dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Western dialects")]

The [Southwest Finnish dialects](/wiki/Southwest_Finnish_dialects "Southwest Finnish dialects") (*lounaissuomalaismurteet*) are spoken in [Southwest Finland](/wiki/Southwest_Finland "Southwest Finland") and [Satakunta](/wiki/Satakunta_(region) "Satakunta (region)"). Their typical feature is abbreviation of word-final vowels, and in many respects they resemble Estonian. The Tavastian dialects (*hämäläismurteet*) are spoken in [Tavastia](/wiki/Tavastia_(historical_province) "Tavastia (historical province)"). They are closest to the standard language, but feature some slight vowel changes, such as the opening of diphthong-final vowels (*tie* → *tiä*, *miekka* → *miakka*, *kuolisi* → *kualis*), the change of d to l (mostly obsolete) or trilled r (widespread, nowadays disappearance of d is popular) and the personal pronouns (*me: meitin* ('we: our'), *te: teitin* ('you: your') and *he: heitin* ('they: their')). [The South Ostrobothnian dialects](/wiki/South_Ostrobothnian_dialect "South Ostrobothnian dialect") (*eteläpohjalaismurteet*) are spoken in [Southern Ostrobothnia](/wiki/Southern_Ostrobothnia "Southern Ostrobothnia"). Their most notable feature is the pronunciation of "d" as a tapped or even fully trilled /r/. The Central and North Ostrobothnian dialects (*keski- ja pohjoispohjalaismurteet*) are spoken in [Central](/wiki/Central_Ostrobothnia "Central Ostrobothnia") and [Northern Ostrobothnia](/wiki/Northern_Ostrobothnia "Northern Ostrobothnia"). The Lapland dialects (*lappilaismurteet*) are spoken in [Lapland](/wiki/Lapland,_Finland "Lapland, Finland"). The dialects spoken in the western parts of Lapland are recognizable by retention of old "h" sounds in positions where they have disappeared from other dialects.

One form of speech related to Northern dialects, [Meänkieli](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nkieli "Meänkieli"), which is spoken on the Swedish side of the border is recognized in Sweden as its own distinct language, having its own standardized language separate from Finnish.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) This form of speech developed from the border created between Sweden and Finland in 1809 when the Russian Empire annexed Finland. This caused the speakers of Meänkieli to be isolated from the developments of standard Finnish and instead be influenced by the Swedish language. However, it is still mutually intelligible with Finnish, and is thus sometimes considered a dialect of the Finnish language.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The [Kven language](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language") is spoken in [Finnmark](/wiki/Finnmark "Finnmark") and [Troms](/wiki/Troms "Troms"), in Norway. Its speakers are descendants of Finnish emigrants to the region in the 18th and 19th centuries. [Kven](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language") is an official minority language in Norway.

A sign in Savonian dialect: "You don't get cognac here, but fresh wheat buns and good strong *Juhla Mokka*-brand coffee you will have. Welcome."

The Eastern dialects consist of the widespread Savonian dialects (*savolaismurteet*) spoken in [Savo](/wiki/Savonia_(historical_province) "Savonia (historical province)") and nearby areas, and the South-Eastern dialects now spoken only in Finnish [South Karelia](/wiki/South_Karelia "South Karelia"). The South Karelian dialects (*eteläkarjalaismurteet*) were previously also spoken on the [Karelian Isthmus](/wiki/Karelian_Isthmus "Karelian Isthmus") and in [Ingria](/wiki/Ingria "Ingria"). The Karelian Isthmus was evacuated during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and refugees were resettled all over Finland. Most [Ingrian Finns](/wiki/Ingrian_Finns "Ingrian Finns") were [deported](/wiki/Deportations_of_the_Ingrian_Finns "Deportations of the Ingrian Finns") to various interior areas of the Soviet Union.

[Palatalization](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)"), a common feature of Uralic languages, had been lost in the Finnic branch, but it has been reacquired by most of these languages, including Eastern Finnish, but not Western Finnish. In Finnish orthography, this is denoted with a "j", e.g. *vesj* [vesʲ] "water", cf. standard *vesi* [vesi].

### Helsinki slang (*Stadin slangi*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Helsinki slang (Stadin slangi)")]

The first known written account in [Helsinki slang](/wiki/Helsinki_slang "Helsinki slang") is from the 1890 short story **Hellaassa** by young Santeri Ivalo (words that do not exist in, or deviate from, the standard spoken Finnish of its time are in bold):

> *Kun minä eilen illalla palasin **labbiksesta**, tapasin **Aasiksen** kohdalla **Supiksen**, ja niin me laskeusimme tänne **Espikselle**, jossa oli mahoton hyvä **piikis**. Mutta me mentiin **Studikselle** suoraan **Hudista** tapaamaan, ja jäimme sinne pariksi tunniksi, kunnes ajoimme **[Kaisikseen](/wiki/Kaisaniemi "Kaisaniemi")**.*[[43]](#cite_note-slanginet-43)

Example of a participle construction; the sign says: "Parking for reserved spaces only."

There are two main [registers](/wiki/Register_(sociolinguistics) "Register (sociolinguistics)") of Finnish used throughout the country. One is the "standard language" (*yleiskieli*), and the other is the "[spoken language](/wiki/Colloquial_Finnish "Colloquial Finnish")" (*puhekieli*). The standard language is used in formal situations like political speeches and newscasts. Its written form, the "book language" (*kirjakieli*), is used in nearly all written texts, not always excluding even the dialogue of common people in popular prose.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The spoken language, on the other hand, is the main variety of Finnish used in popular TV and radio shows and at workplaces, and may be preferred to a dialect in personal communication.

### Standardization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Standardization")]

Standard Finnish is prescribed by the Language Office of the [Research Institute for the Languages of Finland](/wiki/Research_Institute_for_the_Languages_of_Finland "Research Institute for the Languages of Finland") and is the language used in official communication. [The Dictionary of Contemporary Finnish](/wiki/Nykysuomen_sanakirja "Nykysuomen sanakirja") (*Nykysuomen sanakirja* 1951–61), with 201,000 entries, was a [prescriptive](/wiki/Prescriptive "Prescriptive") dictionary that defined official language. An additional volume for words of foreign origin (*Nykysuomen sivistyssanakirja*, 30,000 entries) was published in 1991. An updated dictionary, [The New Dictionary of Modern Finnish](/wiki/The_New_Dictionary_of_Modern_Finnish "The New Dictionary of Modern Finnish") (*Kielitoimiston sanakirja*) was published in an electronic form in 2004 and in print in 2006. A [descriptive](/wiki/Linguistic_description "Linguistic description") grammar (the [Large grammar of Finnish](/wiki/Iso_suomen_kielioppi "Iso suomen kielioppi"), *Iso suomen kielioppi*,[[48]](#cite_note-48) 1,600 pages) was published in 2004. There is also an etymological dictionary, *Suomen sanojen alkuperä*, published in 1992–2000, and a handbook of contemporary language (*Nykysuomen käsikirja*). Standard Finnish is used in official texts and is the form of language taught in schools. Its spoken form is used in political speech, newscasts, in courts, and in other formal situations. Nearly all publishing and printed works are in standard Finnish.

### Colloquial Finnish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Colloquial Finnish")]

Main article: [Colloquial Finnish](/wiki/Colloquial_Finnish "Colloquial Finnish")

As is common among languages with a long-standing standardized form, general Finnish show signs of certain commonplace supradialectal colloquial forms. For example, irregular verbs have developed in the spoken language as a result of the [elision](/wiki/Elision "Elision") of [sonorants](/wiki/Sonorant "Sonorant") in some verbs of the [Type III](/wiki/Finnish_verb_conjugation#Type_III_verbs "Finnish verb conjugation") class (with subsequent vowel [assimilation](/wiki/Assimilation_(phonology) "Assimilation (phonology)")), but only when the second syllable of the word is short. The result is that some forms in the spoken language are shortened, e.g. *tule-n* → *tuu-n* ('I come'), while others remain identical to the standard language *hän tulee* "he comes", never \**hän tuu*). However, the longer forms such as *tule* can be used in spoken language in other forms as well.

A prominent example of the effect of the standard language is the development of the consonant gradation form /ts : ts/ as in *metsä : metsän*, as this pattern was originally (1940) found natively only in the dialects of the southern Karelian isthmus and [Ingria](/wiki/Ingria "Ingria"). It has been reinforced by the spelling "ts" for the dental fricative [θː], used earlier in some western dialects. The spelling and the pronunciation this encourages however approximate the original pronunciation, still reflected in e.g. [Karelian](/wiki/Karelian_language "Karelian language") /čč : č/ (*meččä : mečän*). In the spoken language, a fusion of Western /tt : tt/ (*mettä : mettän*) and Eastern /ht : t/ (*mehtä : metän*) has resulted in /tt : t/ (*mettä : metän*).[[49]](#cite_note-49) Neither of these forms are identifiable as, or originate from, a specific dialect.

The orthography of informal language follows that of the formal. However, in signalling the former in writing, [syncope](/wiki/Syncope_(phonology) "Syncope (phonology)") and [sandhi](/wiki/Sandhi "Sandhi") – especially internal – may occasionally amongst other characteristics be transcribed, e.g. *menenpä → me(n)empä*.

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Main article: [Finnish phonology](/wiki/Finnish_phonology "Finnish phonology")

### Segmental phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Segmental phonology")]

The phoneme inventory of Finnish is moderately small,[[50]](#cite_note-50) with a great number of vocalic segments and a restricted set of consonant types, both of which can be long or short.

#### Vocalic segments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Vocalic segments")]

Finnish monophthongs show eight vowel qualities that contrast in duration. Vowel [allophony](/wiki/Allophony "Allophony") is quite restricted. All vowels are possible in both initial and non-initial syllables, whether long or short. Long and short vowels are shown below.

|  | Front | | Back |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Unrounded | Rounded |
| Close | [i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") [iː](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") | [y](/wiki/Close_front_rounded_vowel "Close front rounded vowel") [yː](/wiki/Close_front_rounded_vowel "Close front rounded vowel") | [u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") [uː](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") |
| Mid | [e](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel") [eː](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel") | [ø](/wiki/Close-mid_front_rounded_vowel "Close-mid front rounded vowel") [øː](/wiki/Close-mid_front_rounded_vowel "Close-mid front rounded vowel") | [o](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel") [oː](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel") |
| Open | [æ](/wiki/Near-open_front_unrounded_vowel "Near-open front unrounded vowel") [æː](/wiki/Near-open_front_unrounded_vowel "Near-open front unrounded vowel") |  | [ɑ](/wiki/Open_back_unrounded_vowel "Open back unrounded vowel") [ɑː](/wiki/Open_back_unrounded_vowel "Open back unrounded vowel") |

The quality of long vowels mostly overlaps with the quality of short vowels, with the exception of **u**, which is centralized with respect to **uu**; long vowels do not morph into [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"). There are eighteen diphthongs; like vowels, diphthongs do not have significant allophony.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-51)** The short [velar nasal](/wiki/Velar_nasal "Velar nasal") only occurs in the sequence /ŋk/ in native vocabulary (where it could alternatively be analysed as an allophone of /n/), and the long velar nasal /ŋŋ/, written ⟨ng⟩, is the equivalent of /ŋk/ under weakening [consonant gradation](/wiki/Consonant_gradation "Consonant gradation") (type of [lenition](/wiki/Lenition "Lenition")) and thus occurs only medially, e.g. *[Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki")* – *Helsingin kaupunki* (city *of Helsinki*) /helsiŋki – helsiŋŋin/.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-52)** /[d](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_plosive "Voiced alveolar plosive")/ is the equivalent of /[t](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_plosive "Voiceless alveolar plosive")/ under weakening [consonant gradation](/wiki/Consonant_gradation "Consonant gradation"), and thus in inherited vocabulary only occurs medially. Especially when spoken by older people, it is often more of an [alveolar](/wiki/Dental_and_alveolar_flaps#Alveolar "Dental and alveolar flaps") [tap](/wiki/Flap_consonant "Flap consonant") than a true voiced stop, and the dialectal realization varies widely; see the main article on [Finnish phonology](/wiki/Finnish_phonology "Finnish phonology").

Almost all consonants have phonemic short and long ([geminated](/wiki/Geminate "Geminate")) forms, although length is only contrastive in medial positions.
Homosyllabic consonant clusters are mostly absent from native Finnish words, except for a small set of two-consonant sequences in [syllable codas](/wiki/Syllable_coda "Syllable coda"), e.g. ⟨rs⟩ in *karsta*. However, as many recently adopted loanwords contain clusters, e.g. *strutsi* from Swedish *struts*, ('ostrich'), they have been integrated to the modern language in varying degrees.

Finnish is somewhat divergent from other Uralic languages in two respects: it has lost most of its fricatives and lost the distinction between [palatalized](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)") and non-palatalized consonants. Finnish has only two fricatives in native words, /s/ and /h/. All other fricatives are recognized as foreign, of which Finnish speakers can usually reliably distinguish /f/ and /ʃ/. The alphabet includes ⟨z⟩, usually realized as the [affricate](/wiki/Affricate "Affricate") [ts], as in German.

While standard Finnish has lost [palatalization](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)"), characteristic of Uralic languages, the eastern dialects and the Karelian language have redeveloped it. For example, the [Karelian](/wiki/Karelian_language "Karelian language") word *d'uuri* [dʲuːri], with a palatalized /dʲ/, is reflected by *juuri* in Finnish and [Savo dialect](/wiki/Savo_dialect "Savo dialect") *vesj* [vesʲ] is *vesi* in standard Finnish.

The phoneme /h/ can vary allophonically between [ç~x~h~ɦ] i.e. *vihko* ['ʋiçko̞], *kahvi* ['kɑxʋi], *raha* ['rɑɦɑ].

A feature of Finnic phonology is the development of labial and rounded vowels in non-initial syllables, as in the word *tyttö*. [Proto-Uralic](/wiki/Proto-Uralic "Proto-Uralic") had only "a", "ä" and "i" in non-initial syllables; modern Finnish allows other vowels in non-initial syllables, although they are less common.

### Prosody

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Prosody")]

Characteristic features of Finnish (common to some other Uralic languages) are [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony") and an [agglutinative](/wiki/Agglutinative "Agglutinative") morphology; owing to the extensive use of the latter, words can be quite long.

The main stress is always on the first syllable, and is in average speech articulated by adding approximately 100 ms more length to the stressed vowel.[[51]](#cite_note-53) Stress does not cause any measurable modifications in vowel quality (very much unlike English). However, stress is not strong and words appear evenly stressed. In some cases, stress is so weak that the highest points of volume, pitch and other indicators of "articulation intensity" are not on the first syllable, although native speakers recognize the first syllable as being stressed.

## Morphophonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Morphophonology")]

Further information: [Finnish consonant gradation](/wiki/Finnish_consonant_gradation "Finnish consonant gradation")

Finnish has several morphophonological processes that require modification of the forms of words for daily speech. The most important processes are [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony") and [consonant gradation](/wiki/Consonant_gradation "Consonant gradation").

Vowel harmony is a redundancy feature, which means that the feature [±back] is uniform within a word, and so it is necessary to interpret it only once for a given word. It is meaning-distinguishing in the initial syllable, and suffixes follow; so, if the listener hears [±back] in any part of the word, they can derive [±back] for the initial syllable. For example, from the stem *tuote* ('product') one derives *t**uo**tteeseens**a*** ('into his product'), where the final vowel becomes the back vowel "a" (rather than the front vowel "ä") because the initial syllable contains the back vowels "uo". This is especially notable because vowels "a" and "ä" are different, meaning-distinguishing [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme"), not interchangeable or [allophonic](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone"). Finnish front vowels are not [umlauts](/wiki/Germanic_umlaut "Germanic umlaut"), though the [graphemes](/wiki/Grapheme "Grapheme") ⟨ä⟩ and ⟨ö⟩ feature [dieresis](/wiki/Diaeresis_(diacritic) "Diaeresis (diacritic)").

Consonant gradation is a partly nonproductive[[52]](#cite_note-54) [lenition](/wiki/Lenition "Lenition") process for P, T and K in inherited vocabulary, with the oblique stem "weakened" from the nominative stem, or vice versa. For example, *tar**kk**a* 'precise' has the oblique stem *tar**k**a-*, as in *tarkan* 'of the precise'. There is also another gradation pattern, which is older, and causes simple elision of T and K in suffixes. However, it is very common since it is found in the partitive case marker: if V is a single vowel, V+*ta* → Va, e.g. \**tarkka+ta* → *tarkkaa*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Multispace_parking_meter_keyboard_%2843594392672%29.jpg/250px-Multispace_parking_meter_keyboard_%2843594392672%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Multispace_parking_meter_keyboard_(43594392672).jpg)

[Parking meter](/wiki/Parking_meter "Parking meter") keyboard with the Finnish alphabet

Finnish is written with the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") including the distinct characters ⟨ä⟩ and ⟨ö⟩, and also several characters (⟨b, c, f, q, w, x, z, å, š⟩ and ⟨ž⟩) reserved for words of non-Finnish origin. The Finnish orthography follows the phonemic principle: each phoneme (meaningful sound) of the language corresponds to exactly one grapheme (independent letter), and each grapheme represents almost exactly one phoneme. This enables an easy spelling and facilitates reading and writing acquisition. The rule of thumb for Finnish orthography is *write as you read, read as you write*. However, morphemes retain their spelling despite [sandhi](/wiki/Sandhi "Sandhi").

Some orthographical notes:

* Long vowels and consonants are represented by double occurrences of the relevant graphemes. This causes no confusion, and permits these sounds to be written without having to nearly double the size of the alphabet to accommodate separate graphemes for long sounds. If a sequence of two identical vowels in different syllables occurs, it is written with an apostrophe, e.g. *rei'itin* "hole punch".
* The grapheme ⟨h⟩ covers all the allophones of /h/. In some positions, it has a fricative quality, which can be [voiced glottal](/wiki/Voiced_glottal_fricative "Voiced glottal fricative") or voiceless [velar](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative") or [palatal](/wiki/Voiceless_palatal_fricative "Voiceless palatal fricative"). This occurs after or between vowels, as in e.g. *lahti*, which is pronounced [lɑxti] with a [voiceless velar fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative").
* [Sandhi](/wiki/Sandhi "Sandhi") is not transcribed; the spelling of morphemes is immutable, such as *tulen+pa* [tulempa].
* Some consonants (⟨v, j, d⟩) do not have distinctive length (and consonant length generally is only contrastive in certain positions), and consequently their allophonic variation is typically not specified in spelling; e.g. *rajaan* [rajaan] ('I limit') vs. *raijaan* [raijjaan] ('I haul').
* Pre-1900s texts and personal names use ⟨w⟩ for ⟨v⟩. Both correspond to the same phoneme, the [labiodental approximant](/wiki/Labiodental_approximant "Labiodental approximant") /ʋ/, a ⟨v⟩ without the fricative ("hissing") quality of the English ⟨v⟩.
* The letters ⟨[ä](/wiki/%C3%84 "Ä")⟩ [æ] and ⟨[ö](/wiki/%C3%96 "Ö")⟩ [ø], although written with [two dots](/wiki/Two_dots_(diacritic) "Two dots (diacritic)"), do not represent [phonological umlauts](/wiki/I-mutation "I-mutation") (as in German, for example), and they are considered independent graphemes; the letter shapes have been copied from Swedish. An appropriate parallel from the Latin alphabet are the characters ⟨C⟩ and ⟨G⟩ (uppercase), which historically have a closer kinship than many other characters (⟨G⟩ is a derivation of ⟨C⟩) but are considered distinct letters, and changing one for the other will change meanings.

Although Finnish orthography is mostly [shallow](/wiki/Shallow_orthography "Shallow orthography"), there are a few differences:

* The ⟨n⟩ in the sequence ⟨nk⟩ is pronounced as a [velar nasal](/wiki/Velar_nasal "Velar nasal") /ŋ/, as in English. When not followed by ⟨k⟩, /ŋː/ is written ⟨ng⟩. The fact that two spellings correspond to this one sound (putting aside the difference in [length](/wiki/Consonant_length "Consonant length")) can be seen as an exception to the general one-to-one correspondence between sounds and letters.
* [Sandhi](/wiki/Sandhi "Sandhi") phenomena at word or clitic boundaries involving gemination (e.g., *tule tänne* is pronounced [tu.le**t**.**t**æn.ne], not [tu.le.tæn.ne]) or the [place assimilation](/wiki/Assimilation_(phonology) "Assimilation (phonology)") of [nasals](/wiki/Nasal_consonant "Nasal consonant") (*sen pupu* would usually be pronounced as [se**m**.pu.pu], and *onpa* as [o**m**.pɑ])
* The /j/ after the letter ⟨i⟩ is very weak or there is no /j/ at all, but in writing it is used; for example: *urheilija*. Indeed, the ⟨j⟩ is not used in writing words with consonant gradation such as *aion* and *läksiäiset*.

When the appropriate characters are not available, the graphemes ⟨ä⟩ and ⟨ö⟩ are usually converted to ⟨a⟩ and ⟨o⟩, respectively. This is common in e-mail addresses and other electronic media where there may be no support for characters outside the basic [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII") character set. Writing them as ⟨ae⟩ and ⟨oe⟩, following German usage, is rarer and usually considered incorrect, but formally used in passports and equivalent situations. Both conversion rules have minimal pairs which would no longer be distinguished from each other.

The sounds ⟨š⟩ and ⟨ž⟩ are not a part of the Finnish language itself and have been introduced by the Finnish national languages body for more phonologically accurate transcription of loanwords (such as *Tšekki*, '[Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic")') and foreign names. For technical reasons or convenience, the graphemes ⟨sh⟩ and ⟨zh⟩ are often used in quickly or less carefully written texts instead of ⟨š⟩ and ⟨ž⟩. This is a deviation from the phonetic principle, and as such is liable to cause confusion, but the damage is minimal as the transcribed words are foreign in any case. Finnish does not use the sounds ⟨z⟩, ⟨š⟩ or ⟨ž⟩, but for the sake of exactitude, they can be included in spelling. (The recommendation cites the Russian opera *[Hovanštšina](/wiki/Khovanshchina "Khovanshchina")* as an example.) Many speakers pronounce all of them ⟨s⟩, or distinguish only between ⟨s⟩ and ⟨š⟩, because Finnish has no voiced sibilants.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

The language may be identified by its distinctive lack of the letters ⟨b, c, f, q, w, x, z⟩ and ⟨å⟩.

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Finnish grammar](/wiki/Finnish_grammar "Finnish grammar")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/En_vittu_tii%C3%A4.jpg/250px-En_vittu_tii%C3%A4.jpg)](/wiki/File:En_vittu_tii%C3%A4.jpg)

An example of the versatility of Finnish inflection. The label of this beer bottle reads *Palaisiko eksän luokse vai helvetissä - en vittu tiiä*, meaning "Should I return to my ex or burn in Hell - I don't fucking know". The same word *palaisiko* can mean either "should I return" or "should I burn" depending on whether it is inflected from *palata* ("to return") or from *palaa* ("to burn").

Finnish is a [synthetic language](/wiki/Synthetic_language "Synthetic language") that employs extensive [agglutination](/wiki/Agglutination "Agglutination") of affixes to verbs, nouns, adjectives and numerals. However, Finnish is not generally considered [polysynthetic](/wiki/Polysynthetic_language "Polysynthetic language"), its morpheme-to-word ratio being somewhat lower than a prototypical polysynthetic language (e.g., [Yup'ik](/wiki/Central_Alaskan_Yup%27ik_language "Central Alaskan Yup'ik language")).[[54]](#cite_note-56)

The [morphosyntactic alignment](/wiki/Morphosyntactic_alignment "Morphosyntactic alignment") of Finnish is nominative–accusative, but there are two object [cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case"): accusative and partitive. The contrast between accusative and partitive [object](/wiki/Grammatical_object "Grammatical object") cases is one of [telicity](/wiki/Telicity "Telicity"), where the accusative case denotes actions completed as intended (*Ammuin hirven* 'I shot the/an elk (dead)'), and the partitive case denotes incomplete actions (*Ammuin hirveä* 'I shot (at) the/an elk').[[55]](#cite_note-57) Often telicity is confused with [perfectivity](/wiki/Perfective_aspect "Perfective aspect"), but these are distinct notions. Finnish in fact has a [periphrastic](/wiki/Periphrasis "Periphrasis") perfective aspect, which in addition to the two inflectional tenses (past and present), yield a [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages")-like system consisting of four tense-aspect combinations: simple present, simple past, [perfect](/wiki/Perfect_(grammar) "Perfect (grammar)") (present + perfective aspect) and [pluperfect](/wiki/Pluperfect "Pluperfect") (past + perfective aspect). No morphological future tense is needed; context and the telicity contrast in object grammatical case serve to disambiguate present events from future events. For example, *syön kalan* 'I eat a fish (completely)' must denote a future event, since there is no way to completely eat a fish at the current moment (the moment the eating is complete, the simple past tense or the perfect must be used). By contrast, *syön kalaa* 'I eat a fish (not yet complete)' denotes a present event by indicating ongoing action.

Finnish has three grammatical [persons](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person"); [finite](/wiki/Finite_verb "Finite verb") verbs [agree](/wiki/Agreement_(linguistics) "Agreement (linguistics)") with subject nouns in person and number by way of suffixes. The (dictionary form) infinitive bears the suffix *-ta/-tä* (often [lenited](/wiki/Lenition "Lenition") to *-(d)a/-(d)ä* due to [consonant gradation](/wiki/Finnish_consonant_gradation "Finnish consonant gradation")).[[56]](#cite_note-58) There is a so-called "passive voice" (sometimes called impersonal or indefinite) which differs from a true passive in various respects.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Transitivity is distinguished in the [derivational](/wiki/Derivational_morpheme "Derivational morpheme") morphology of verbs, e.g. *ratkaista* 'to solve something' vs. *ratketa* 'to solve by itself'. There are also several [frequentative](/wiki/Frequentative "Frequentative") and [momentane](/wiki/Momentane "Momentane") affixes which form new verbs derivationally.

## Lexicon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Lexicon")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Suomalaisen_Sana-Lugun_Coetus.jpg/250px-Suomalaisen_Sana-Lugun_Coetus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suomalaisen_Sana-Lugun_Coetus.jpg)

*[Suomalaisen Sana-Lugun Coetus](/wiki/Suomalaisen_Sana-Lugun_Coetus "Suomalaisen Sana-Lugun Coetus")* (1745) by [Daniel Juslenius](/wiki/Daniel_Juslenius "Daniel Juslenius") was the first comprehensive dictionary of the Finnish language with 16,000 entries.

Finnish has a smaller core vocabulary than, for example, English, and uses [derivational](/wiki/Derivational_morpheme "Derivational morpheme") suffixes to a greater extent. As an example, take the word *kirja* "a book", from which one can form derivatives *kirjain* 'a letter' (of the [alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet "Alphabet")), *kirje* 'a piece of correspondence, a letter', *kirjasto* 'a library', *kirjailija* 'an author', *kirjallisuus* 'literature', *kirjoittaa* 'to write', *kirjoittaja* 'a writer', *kirjuri* 'a scribe, a clerk', *kirjallinen* 'in written form', *kirjata* 'to write down, register, record', *kirjasin* 'a font', and many others.

Here are some of the more common such suffixes. Which of each pair is used depends on the word being suffixed in accordance with the rules of [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony").

Examples of Finnish derivational suffixes on nouns

| Suffix | Used to create... | Example(s) | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| -*ja / -jä* | [agents](/wiki/Agent_noun "Agent noun") from verbs | *lukea* 'to read' → *lukija* 'reader' |  |
| *-sto / -stö* | [collective nouns](/wiki/Collective_noun "Collective noun") | *kirja* 'a book' → *kirjasto* 'a library' *laiva* 'a ship' → *laivasto* 'navy, fleet' |  |
| *-in* | instruments or tools | *kirjata* 'to book, to file' → *kirjain* 'a letter' (of the alphabet)  *vatkata* 'to whisk' → *vatkain* 'a whisk, mixer' |  |
| *-uri / -yri* | [agents](/wiki/Agent_(grammar) "Agent (grammar)") or instruments | *kaivaa* 'to dig' → *kaivuri* 'an excavator' *laiva* 'a ship' → *laivuri* 'shipper, shipmaster' |  |
| *-os / -ös* | [result](http://www.glottopedia.org/index.php/Result_nominal#:~:text=A%20result%20nominal%20is%20a,verb%20it%20is%20derived%20from.) nouns from verbs | *tulla* 'to come' → *tulos* 'result, outcome' *tehdä* 'to do' → *teos* 'a piece of work' |  |
| *-ton / -tön* | [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") indicating the lack of something | *onni* 'happiness' → *onneton* 'unhappy' *koti* 'home' → *koditon* 'homeless' |  |
| *-kas / -käs* | adjectives from nouns | *itse* 'self' → *itsekäs* 'selfish' *neuvo* 'advice' → *neuvokas* 'resourceful' |  |
| *-va / -vä* | adjectives from verbs | *taitaa* 'to be able' → *taitava* 'skillful' *johtaa* 'to lead' → *johtava* 'leading' |  |
| *-llinen* | adjectives from nouns | *lapsi* 'child' → *lapsellinen* 'childish' *kauppa* 'a shop, commerce' → *kaupallinen* 'commercial' |  |
| *-la / -lä* | locations (places related to the stem) | *kana* 'a hen' → *kanala* 'a henhouse' *pappi* 'a priest' → *pappila* 'a parsonage' |  |
| *-lainen / -läinen* | inhabitants (of places), among others | *Englanti* 'England' → *englantilainen* 'English person/thing' *Venäjä* 'Russia' → *venäläinen* 'Russian person or thing'. | formed from *-la / -lä* plus *[-inen](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-inen "wiktionary:-inen")* |

Verbal derivational suffixes are extremely diverse; several [frequentatives](/wiki/Frequentative "Frequentative") and [momentanes](/wiki/Momentane "Momentane") differentiating [causative](/wiki/Causative "Causative"), volitional-unpredictable and [anticausative](/wiki/Anticausative "Anticausative") are found, often combined with each other, often denoting indirection. For example, *hypätä* 'to jump', *hyppiä* 'to be jumping', *hypeksiä* 'to be jumping wantonly', *hypäyttää* 'to make someone jump once', *hyppyyttää* 'to make someone jump repeatedly' (or 'to boss someone around'), *hyppyytyttää* 'to make someone to cause a third person to jump repeatedly', *hyppyytellä* 'to, without aim, make someone jump repeatedly', *hypähtää* 'to jump suddenly' (in [anticausative](/wiki/Anticausative "Anticausative") meaning), *hypellä* 'to jump around repeatedly', *hypiskellä* 'to be jumping repeatedly and wantonly'. [Caritives](/wiki/Caritive "Caritive") are also used in such examples as *hyppimättä* 'without jumping' and *hyppelemättä* 'without jumping around'. The diversity and compactness of both derivation and inflectional agglutination can be illustrated with *istahtaisinkohankaan* 'I wonder if I should sit down for a while after all' (from *istua*, 'to sit, to be seated'):

* *istua* 'to sit down' (*istun* 'I sit down')
* *istahtaa* 'to sit down for a while'
* *istahdan* 'I'll sit down for a while'
* *istahtaisin* 'I would sit down for a while'
* *istahtaisinko* 'should I sit down for a while?'
* *istahtaisinkohan* 'I wonder if I should sit down for a while'
* *istahtaisinkohankaan* 'I wonder if I should sit down for a while after all'

### Borrowing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finnish_language&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Borrowing")]

Over the course of many centuries, the Finnish language has borrowed many words from a wide variety of languages, most from neighbouring [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"). Owing to the different grammatical, phonological and phonotactic structure of the Finnish language, loanwords from Indo-European have been assimilated.

While early borrowings, possibly even into [Proto-Uralic](/wiki/Proto-Uralic "Proto-Uralic"), from very early [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") can be found, Finnic languages, including Finnish, have borrowed in particular from Baltic and Germanic languages, and to a lesser extent from Slavic and Indo-Iranian languages (all of which are subgroupings of Indo-European). Furthermore, [a certain group](/wiki/Pre-Finno-Ugric_substrate "Pre-Finno-Ugric substrate") of very basic and neutral words exists in Finnish and other Finnic languages that are absent from other Uralic languages, but without a recognizable etymology from any known language. These words are usually regarded[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] as the last remnant of the [Paleo-European](/wiki/Paleo-European_languages "Paleo-European languages") language spoken in Fennoscandia before the arrival of the proto-Finnic language.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Words included in this group are e.g. *jänis* (hare), *musta* (black), *saari* (island), *suo* (swamp) and *niemi* (cape (geography)).

Also some place names, like [Päijänne](/wiki/P%C3%A4ij%C3%A4nne "Päijänne") and [Imatra](/wiki/Imatra "Imatra"), are probably from before the proto-Finnic era.[[58]](#cite_note-60)

Often quoted loan examples are *kuningas* 'king' and *ruhtinas* '[sovereign prince](/wiki/Prince "Prince"), high ranking nobleman' from Germanic \**kuningaz* and \**druhtinaz*—they display a remarkable tendency towards phonological conservation within the language. Another example is *äiti* 'mother' (from Germanic \**aiþį̄*), which is interesting because borrowing of close-kinship vocabulary is a rare phenomenon. The original Finnish *emo* and *emä* occurs only in restricted contexts. There are other close-kinship words that are loaned from Baltic and Germanic languages (*morsian* 'bride', *armas* 'dear', *huora* 'whore'). Examples of the ancient Iranian loans are *vasara* 'hammer' from [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan") *vadžra*, *vajra* and *orja* 'slave' from *[arya](/wiki/Aryan "Aryan")*, *airya* 'man' (the latter probably via similar circumstances as *slave* from [*Slav*](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples") in many European languages[[59]](#cite_note-61)).

More recently, Swedish has been a prolific source of borrowings, and also, the Swedish language acted as a proxy for European words, especially those relating to government. Present-day Finland was a part of Sweden from the 12th century and was ceded to Russia in 1809, becoming an autonomous Grand Duchy. Swedish was retained as the official language and language of the upper class even after this. When Finnish was accepted as an official language, it gained legal equal status with Swedish. During the period of autonomy, Russian did not gain much ground as a language of the people or the government. Nevertheless, quite a few words were subsequently acquired from [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") (especially in older [Helsinki slang](/wiki/Helsinki_slang "Helsinki slang")) but not to the same extent as with Swedish. In all these cases, borrowing has been partly a result of geographical proximity.

Especially words dealing with administrative or modern culture came to Finnish from Swedish, sometimes reflecting the oldest Swedish form of the word (*lag* – *laki*, 'law'; *län* – *[lääni](/wiki/Provinces_of_Finland "Provinces of Finland")*, 'province'; *bisp* – *piispa*, 'bishop'; *jordpäron* – *peruna*, 'potato'), and many more survive as informal synonyms in spoken or dialectal Finnish (e.g. *likka*, from Swedish *flicka*, 'girl', usually *tyttö* in Finnish).

Some Slavic loanwords are old or very old, thus hard to recognize as such, and concern everyday concepts, e.g. *papu* 'bean', *raja* 'border' and *pappi* 'priest'. Notably, a few religious words such as *Raamattu* ('Bible') are borrowed from [Old East Slavic](/wiki/Old_East_Slavic "Old East Slavic"), which indicates language contact preceding the Swedish era. This is mainly believed to be result of trade with Novgorod from the 9th century on and [Russian Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox "Russian Orthodox") [missions](/wiki/Mission_(Christianity) "Mission (Christianity)") in the east in the 13th century.

Most recently, and with increasing impact, English has been the source of new [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") in Finnish. Unlike previous geographical borrowing, the influence of English is largely cultural and reaches Finland by many routes, including international business, music, film and TV (foreign films and programmes, excluding ones intended for a very young audience, are shown subtitled), literature, and the [Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web") – the latter is now probably the most important source of all non-face-to-face exposure to English.

The importance of English as the language of global commerce has led many non-English companies, including Finland's [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia"), to adopt English as their official operating language. Recently, it has been observed that English borrowings are also ousting previous borrowings, for example the switch from *treffailla* 'to date' (from Swedish, *träffa*) to *deittailla* from English 'to go for a date'. [Calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque") from English are also found, e.g. *kovalevy* (hard disk), and so are grammatical calques, for example, the replacement of the impersonal (*passiivi*) with the English-style [generic you](/wiki/Generic_you "Generic you"), e. g. *sä et voi* 'you cannot', instead of the proper impersonal *ei voida* 'one cannot' or impersonal third-person singular *ei voi* 'one cannot'. This construct, however, is limited to colloquial language, as it is against the standard grammar.

English loan words in Finnish slang include for example *pleikkari* 'PlayStation', *hodari* 'hot dog', and *hedari* 'headache', 'headshot' or 'headbutt'. Often these loanwords are distinctly identified as [slang](/wiki/Slang "Slang") or [jargon](/wiki/Jargon "Jargon"), rarely being used in a negative mood or in formal language. Most loan words are inevitably sooner or later [calqued](/wiki/Calque "Calque") – translated into native Finnish – retaining the semantic meaning.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Moreover, neologisms are coined actively not only by the government, but also by the media.

Some modern terms have been synthesised rather than borrowed, for example:

:   *puhelin* 'telephone' (from the stem *puhel-* 'talk' + instrument suffix *-in* to make 'an instrument for talking')
:   *tietokone* 'computer' (literally: 'knowledge machine' or 'data machine')
:   *levyke* 'diskette' (from *levy* 'disc' + a diminutive *-ke*)
:   *sähköposti* 'email' (literally: 'electricity mail')
:   *linja-auto* 'bus, coach' (literally: line-car)
:   *muovi* 'plastic' (from *muovata* 'to mould, form or model, e.g. from clay'; compare *plastic* from Ancient Greek πλᾰστῐκός (*plastikós*) 'mouldable, fit for moulding')

Neologisms are actively generated by the Language Planning Office and the media. They are widely adopted. One would actually give an old-fashioned or rustic impression using forms such as *kompuutteri* (computer) or *kalkulaattori* (calculator) when the neologism is widely adopted.

Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights"):

:   *Kaikki ihmiset syntyvät vapaina ja tasavertaisina arvoltaan ja oikeuksiltaan. Heille on annettu järki ja omatunto, ja heidän on toimittava toisiaan kohtaan veljeyden hengessä.*
:   "All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood."[[60]](#cite_note-62)

Excerpt from [Väinö Linna](/wiki/V%C3%A4in%C3%B6_Linna "Väinö Linna")'s *[Tuntematon sotilas](/wiki/The_Unknown_Soldier_(novel) "The Unknown Soldier (novel)")* (The Unknown Soldier); these words were also inscribed in the 20 [mark](/wiki/Finnish_markka "Finnish markka") note.

:   *Hyväntahtoinen aurinko katseli heitä. Se ei missään tapauksessa ollut heille vihainen. Kenties tunsi jonkinlaista myötätuntoakin heitä kohtaan. Aika velikultia.*
:   "The sun smiled down on them. It wasn't angry – no, not by any means. Maybe it even felt some sort of sympathy for them. Rather dear, those boys."
